name: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
subject: Physics

sections.heading: 1. Electron Emission
sections.body:
Free electrons in a metal cannot escape at room temperature because of the attractive force of positive ions. The minimum energy required for an electron to just escape from a metal surface is the work function $\phi_0$ (or $W$), measured in eV. Typical values: 2–5 eV.

Methods to supply this energy:

  • Thermionic emission: heating (used in CRT cathodes).
  • Field emission: applying very strong electric field.
  • Photoelectric emission: light of suitable frequency.
  • Secondary emission: bombardment by other particles.

1 eV $= 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J.

sections.heading: 2. Photoelectric Effect — Hertz and Hallwachs Observations
sections.body:
Hertz (1887) discovered that UV light incident on a negatively charged metal causes loss of charge — emission of electrons. Hallwachs and Lenard refined experiments using a vacuum tube with cathode and anode, observing photocurrent.

Key experimental facts:

  • Below a certain threshold frequency $\nu_0$ (specific to the metal), no electrons are emitted, no matter how intense the light.
  • Above $\nu_0$, photoelectrons are emitted instantaneously (within $\sim 10^{-9}$ s).
  • Photocurrent (number of electrons per second) is proportional to light intensity.
  • Maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons depends only on light frequency, not intensity.
  • Stopping potential $V_0$ (reverse voltage to halt fastest photoelectrons) gives $K_{\max} = eV_0$. Stopping potential is independent of intensity.

Classical wave theory cannot explain frequency threshold or instantaneous emission — failure motivated quantum theory.

sections.heading: 3. Einstein's Photoelectric Equation
sections.body:
Einstein (1905, Nobel 1921) explained photoelectric effect by proposing that light consists of discrete packets of energy — photons — each carrying energy $E = h u$, where $h = 6.626 imes 10^{-34}$ J·s is Planck's constant.

One photon is absorbed by one electron. The electron uses energy $\phi_0$ to escape; the rest becomes kinetic energy: $$h u = \phi_0 + K_{\max}$$ Equivalently: $$K_{\max} = h u - \phi_0 = h( u - u_0)$$ where $ u_0 = \phi_0/h$ is the threshold frequency.

This explains all observations:

  • Threshold: if $ u < u_0$, $h u < \phi_0$, electron can't escape regardless of intensity.
  • Instantaneous emission: one photon absorbed in one event.
  • Intensity affects number of photons (hence current), not photon energy.
  • $K_{\max}$ depends linearly on $ u$ (Millikan's experiments confirmed slope $h/e$).

sections.heading: 4. Particle Nature of Light — Photons
sections.body:
The photon picture:

  • Energy: $E = h\nu = hc/\lambda$.
  • Momentum: $p = h\nu/c = h/\lambda$ (rest mass zero, but momentum from relativistic $E = pc$).
  • Travels at $c$ in vacuum; not deflected by electric/magnetic fields.
  • In photon-particle collisions, both energy and momentum conserved.

Light shows wave-like behaviour (interference, diffraction) and particle-like behaviour (photoelectric effect, Compton scattering): wave-particle duality.

sections.heading: 5. de Broglie Hypothesis — Wave Nature of Matter
sections.body:
de Broglie (1924) proposed that matter, like light, has dual nature: a particle of momentum $p$ has an associated wavelength $$\lambda = \dfrac{h}{p} = \dfrac{h}{mv}$$ Wave-like properties are negligible for macroscopic objects (e.g. cricket ball at 50 m/s has $\lambda \sim 10^{-34}$ m) but significant for electrons.

For an electron accelerated through potential $V$: $K = eV = p^2/(2m)$, so $p = \sqrt{2mEV}$, giving $$\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2meV}} = \dfrac{12.27}{\sqrt{V}}\,\mathring{\text{A}}$$ where $V$ is in volts. For a 100 V electron: $\lambda \approx 1.23$ Å — comparable to atomic spacings.

sections.heading: 6. Davisson-Germer Experiment
sections.body:
Davisson and Germer (1927) confirmed the wave nature of electrons: a beam of low-energy electrons scattered off a single nickel crystal showed a sharp peak at scattering angle 50° for 54 V acceleration — interpretable as Bragg diffraction.

Wavelength calculated from de Broglie's formula: $\lambda = 12.27/\sqrt{54}$ Å $\approx 1.67$ Å.

Wavelength calculated from Bragg's law (assuming nickel lattice spacing): $\lambda \approx 1.65$ Å.

Excellent agreement validated de Broglie's hypothesis. This led to electron microscopy (much higher resolution than light microscopes due to shorter $\lambda$).

summary: Photoelectric effect: light above threshold frequency ejects electrons instantly with $K_{\max} = h\nu - \phi_0$. Einstein: light is photons with $E = h\nu$, $p = h/\lambda$. de Broglie: matter has wavelength $\lambda = h/p$; for electrons accelerated by $V$: $\lambda = 12.27/\sqrt{V}$ Å. Davisson-Germer's diffraction of electrons confirmed matter waves. Light and matter both exhibit wave-particle duality.
pitfalls: Stopping potential and K_max relate by K_max = eV_0; do NOT confuse V_0 with photon energy.
Photoelectric effect is INSTANTANEOUS — wave theory predicted a delay (one of its failures).
Threshold frequency is metal-specific; intensity affects current (number of electrons), not max KE.
Photon has zero rest mass but nonzero momentum p = h/λ.
de Broglie wavelength is significant only for very small particles — irrelevant for cricket balls.
λ = 12.27/√V Å only valid for non-relativistic electrons; works fine for ≤ ~10 kV.
